(born February 10, 1975) is an American former [professional basketball](/wiki/Professional_basketball "Professional basketball") player and coach. Most recently, she served as the head coach of the [Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_women%27s_basketball "Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball") team from 2018 to 2022; she was subsequently hired by the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/Portland_Trail_Blazers "Portland Trail Blazers") as a team scout later in 2022.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Thompson was inducted into both the [Women's Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Women%27s_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Women's Basketball Hall of Fame") and the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 2018.

**Tina Marie Thompson** (born February 10, 1975) is an American former [professional basketball](/wiki/Professional_basketball "Professional basketball") player and coach. Most recently, she served as the head coach of the [Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_women%27s_basketball "Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball") team from 2018 to 2022; she was subsequently hired by the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/Portland_Trail_Blazers "Portland Trail Blazers") as a team scout later in 2022.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Thompson was inducted into both the [Women's Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Women%27s_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Women's Basketball Hall of Fame") and the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 2018.

The first college draft pick in [WNBA](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") history, Thompson was selected first by the [Houston Comets](/wiki/Houston_Comets "Houston Comets"). She helped lead the Comets to four consecutive [WNBA Championships](/wiki/WNBA_Finals "WNBA Finals") in 1997, 1998, 1999, and 2000. She won two Olympic gold medals and made nine [WNBA All-Star Game](/wiki/WNBA_All-Star_Game "WNBA All-Star Game") appearances. Until 2017, she was the WNBA's all-time leading scorer and, as of 2026, she [ranks fourth](/wiki/List_of_WNBA_career_scoring_leaders "List of WNBA career scoring leaders") in WNBA history.

## Early life and college

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and college")]

Thompson was born in [Los Angeles, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"). She grew up playing basketball with her brother TJ and his friends at Robertson Park in [West Los Angeles, California](/wiki/West_Los_Angeles,_California "West Los Angeles, California"). She recorded more than 1,500 points and 1,000 rebounds in her high school career at [Morningside High School](/wiki/Morningside_High_School "Morningside High School") in [Inglewood, California](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California"), where she also played [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"). She then went on to play basketball at the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California"), where she graduated in 1997.[[2]](#cite_note-porter183-2) She attended both high school and college with fellow WNBA player [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie").

## USA Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: USA Basketball")]

Thompson represented the US at the 1995 [World University Games](/wiki/World_University_Games "World University Games") held in [Fukuoka, Japan](/wiki/Fukuoka,_Japan "Fukuoka, Japan"), in August and September 1995. The team had a record of 5–1, securing the silver medal. The USA team won early and reached a record of 5–0 when the USA beat Yugoslavia. In the semi-final game, the USA faced Russia. The team was behind much of the first half but tied the game at the half. The USA broke the game open in the second half and won, 101–74. The gold medal match was against unbeaten Italy. The Italian team started strong, scoring 12 of the first 14 points of the contest. [Sylvia Crawley](/wiki/Sylvia_Crawley "Sylvia Crawley") scored eight consecutive points to end the first half, but that left the USA nine points behind. The USA took a small lead in the second half, but the team from Italy responded with a ten-point run, and won the game and the gold medal by a score of 73–65. Thompson averaged 9.9 points per game and was second on the team with 7.3 rebounds per game.[[4]](#cite_note-1995_WUG-4)

Thompson was invited to be a member of the Jones Cup team representing the US in 1996. She helped the team to a 9–0 record, and the gold medal in the event. In the game against Slovakia, which would determine the gold medal, she combined with teammate [Michelle M. Marciniak](/wiki/Michelle_M._Marciniak "Michelle M. Marciniak") to score 30 points in a game they had to come from behind to win 72–62. Thompson averaged 9.6 points per game and 6.2 rebounds, both second highest on the team.[[5]](#cite_note-1996_Jones_Cup-5)

Thompson was selected to be a member of the National team for 1998 World Championships, but was injured and unable to compete.[[6]](#cite_note-1998_WC-6)

Thompson was named to the national team representing the US at the 2006 World Championships, held in [Barueri](/wiki/Barueri "Barueri") and [Sao Paulo, Brazil](/wiki/Sao_Paulo,_Brazil "Sao Paulo, Brazil"). The team won eight of their nine contests, but the lone loss came in the semifinal medal round to Russia. The USA beat Brazil in the final game to earn the bronze medal. Thompson led all scorers with 14.4 points per game. In a game against Russia, she tied a team record by hitting four of four three-point attempts.[[7]](#cite_note-2006_WC-7)

Thompson also played for Team USA in the [2004](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") and [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), winning two Olympic gold medals with the team.

## WNBA career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: WNBA career")]

Thompson was selected No. 1 overall in the first round of the inaugural [1997 WNBA draft](/wiki/1997_WNBA_Draft "1997 WNBA Draft") by the [Houston Comets](/wiki/Houston_Comets "Houston Comets").[[2]](#cite_note-porter183-2) Her debut game was played on June 21, 1997 in a 76 - 56 victory over the [Cleveland Rockers](/wiki/Cleveland_Rockers "Cleveland Rockers"). In her debut, Thompson recorded 14 points, 5 rebounds and 4 blocks.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On the Comets, she was a member of a dynasty along with [Sheryl Swoopes](/wiki/Sheryl_Swoopes "Sheryl Swoopes") and [Cynthia Cooper](/wiki/Cynthia_Cooper-Dyke "Cynthia Cooper-Dyke") that won four consecutive WNBA championships from 1997 to 2000. During her stint with the Comets, Thompson had won All-Star MVP honors at the [2000 WNBA All-Star Game](/wiki/2000_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2000 WNBA All-Star Game"), led all Western Conference players in All-Star voting in 2001, had been named to the All-WNBA First Team three times (1997, 1998, 2004) and All-WNBA Second Team four times (1999, 2000, 2001, 2002).

Prior to the 2005 season, Thompson had given birth to Dyllan Thompson-Jones,[[9]](#cite_note-9) her first child in May, with then [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") player [Damon Jones](/wiki/Damon_Jones "Damon Jones") being the biological father; she resumed playing with the Comets two months later.[[10]](#cite_note-NYT-10) Following her pregnancy she had a sluggish season in 2005, averaging only 10.1 ppg.

In 2006, Thompson returned to peak condition, averaging 18.7 ppg and scored a career-high 37 points in a triple-overtime loss to the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury"). That year the Comets made the playoffs for the final time before folding, after they were eliminated in a two-game sweep by the [Sacramento Monarchs](/wiki/Sacramento_Monarchs "Sacramento Monarchs") in the first round.

After the Comets folded in 2008, Thompson signed with her hometown team, the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks") in 2009, playing alongside [Candace Parker](/wiki/Candace_Parker "Candace Parker") and [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie") who was playing in her final year before retirement.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Thompson, Leslie and Parker led the Sparks to the playoffs with an 18–16 record. In the playoffs, Thompson was one win away from her fifth Finals appearance but the Sparks lost 2–1 in the second round to the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury"), who were the champions that year. During the following season in August, Thompson became the WNBA's all-time leading scorer, passing Lisa Leslie during a regular-season game loss to the [San Antonio Silver Stars](/wiki/San_Antonio_Silver_Stars "San Antonio Silver Stars") in which she scored 23 points.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2011, she was voted in by fans as one of the top 15 players in the fifteen-year history of the WNBA.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

An unrestricted free agent at the end of the 2011 season, Thompson signed with the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") on February 27, 2012, to fill gaps left by Australia's [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson"), concurrent her commitment to the Australian national team for the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), and small forward [Swin Cash](/wiki/Swin_Cash "Swin Cash"), who was traded to the [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky") as part of a package deal for the second-overall pick in the [2012 WNBA draft](/wiki/2012_WNBA_draft "2012 WNBA draft").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On May 31, 2013, Thompson announced that she would retire from the WNBA at the end of the 2013 season.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

During the 2013 season, the 38-year-old Thompson was a starter for the Storm and had averaged 14.1 ppg. She was also selected to the [2013 WNBA All-Star Game](/wiki/2013_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2013 WNBA All-Star Game") to replace an injured [Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was her ninth career WNBA All-Star Game appearance, the second-most in WNBA history, and it also made her the first and only player in WNBA history to be named an All-Star in three different decades.

On August 17, 2013, Thompson became the first WNBA player to have 7,000 points and 3,000 rebounds following a victory against the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") where she scored 23 points and grabbed 7 rebounds.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

September 14, 2013, marked the final regular-season game of Thompson's career which resulted in a victory over the [Tulsa Shock](/wiki/Tulsa_Shock "Tulsa Shock"). Following the game, an almost hour-long retirement ceremony took place in her honor. Her Storm teammates all wore a number 7 jersey either in a Comets or Storm variant. Despite the absence of [Sue Bird](/wiki/Sue_Bird "Sue Bird") who sat out the whole season while recovering from knee surgery, the Storm made the playoffs with the number 4 seed in the Western Conference.

Thompson's final WNBA career game was Game 2 of the first round in the [2013 WNBA Playoffs](/wiki/2013_WNBA_Playoffs "2013 WNBA Playoffs"). The Storm were eliminated in a two-game sweep by the [Minnesota Lynx](/wiki/Minnesota_Lynx "Minnesota Lynx") who would win the championship that year. Thompson recorded 13 points, 9 rebounds and 2 steals in the loss.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2016, Thompson was again honored by the WNBA, being named in the [WNBA Top 20@20](/wiki/WNBA_Top_20@20 "WNBA Top 20@20") in celebration of the league's twentieth anniversary.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

On March 31, 2018, Thompson was named to the 2018 class of inductees for the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International career")]

Thompson was an alternate for the 2000 Olympic squad. Thompson was a member of the U.S. women's basketball team and she earned a gold medal at the Summer Olympic Games: [Beijing 2008](/wiki/Beijing_2008 "Beijing 2008"). She was named to the 1998 and 2002 USA World Championship teams, but injuries kept her from both competitions. She earned her gold medal in 2004 in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece").[[2]](#cite_note-porter183-2)

Thompson has also participated in other professional leagues overseas. Following the 2001 WNBA season, Thompson played for Rovereto Basket in Rovereto, Italy, and in 2003 she played for the Kumho Falcons of the [Women's Korea Basketball League (WKBL)](/wiki/Women%27s_Korean_Basketball_League "Women's Korean Basketball League").

## Coaching career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Coaching career")]

On March 18, 2015, the University of Texas at Austin athletic department announced Thompson's hire as an assistant coach for the Longhorn women's basketball team, beginning her collegiate coaching career.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Two years later, Thompson was promoted to Associate Head Coach with the Lady Longhorns.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

After three seasons with the Lady Longhorns, Thompson was named head coach of the [Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_women%27s_basketball "Virginia Cavaliers women's basketball") program on April 16, 2018.[[25]](#cite_note-25) She was hired by the first [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") female athletics director of any [power conference](/wiki/Power_conference "Power conference") university, [Carla Williams](/wiki/Carla_Williams_(athletic_director) "Carla Williams (athletic director)"). On March 3, 2022, the university announced that Thompson had been relieved of her duties after going 30-63 during her tenure.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tina_Thompson&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Awards and achievements")]

* 4-time WNBA Championship
* 2-time Olympic gold medalist
* 2000 [WNBA All-Star selection](/wiki/2000_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2000 WNBA All-Star Game") (MVP)
* 9-time [WNBA All-Star selection](/wiki/WNBA_All-Star_Game "WNBA All-Star Game")
* Named to the All-WNBA team 8 times
* Member of the [WNBA All-Decade Team](/wiki/WNBA_All-Decade_Team "WNBA All-Decade Team")
* 3rd in WNBA all-time scoring
